text: Perineal sinus is a late complication of radical surgery in the pelvis for both benign disease and cancer. An excision of these sinuses can often result in substantial tissue loss. Previous plastic myofascial flap techniques designed to improve healing in the perineum can often result in substantial late complications such as incisional hernia. Therefore, the use of a fasciocutaneous flap, such as the inferior gluteal artery perforator flap, could allow improved perineal healing with reduced donor site morbidity.
This video aims to describe some technical considerations in use of the eye gap flap for reconstruction in the perineum after substantial tissue loss.

This forty-five-year-old man underwent a single-stage colectomy and formation of an ileal pouch at another institution, with substantial post-operative morbidity. He then came to pouch excision some two years ago and was left with a chronic perineal sinus, which on MRI was confirmed to extend to the coccyx. The perineal sinus, in this case, was ten centimeters deep.

The surgeon begins the operation with a teardrop-shaped incision extending cranially and caudally along the midline. The perineal sinus is dissected down to its base using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery. Assistance is provided to reach the base of the deep cavity. After mobilizing the sinus, the surgeon ensures complete clearance of affected tissue, including excision of the base of the coccyx, before flap placement.

The surgeon begins constructing the eye gap flap by defining a skin paddle centered around the inferior gluteal artery perforation. The incision is made along a line between the posterior superior iliac spine and the ischial tuberosity, at the level of the piriformis. The flap is created by incising through the subcutaneous and fascial layers down to the gluteus maximus muscle, ensuring no damage to underlying structures.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the fasciocutaneous artery perforator (FAP) flap in a lateral to medial direction within the sub-fascial layer, carefully dissecting to avoid damage to the perforating vessels consistently found in this area.

The surgeon identifies perforating vessels and mobilizes them from the gluteus maximus muscle by dissecting along the line of fiber travel. The dissection allows for good rotation of the resulting flap, demonstrating the degree of mobility achieved.

The surgeon advances the flap into the perineal defect, identifying and de-epithelializing the buried portion. Multiple perforating vessels, including an artery and a vein, are visible passing through the gluteus maximus muscle into the eye gap flap. The flap is secured superiorly to the superficial part of the sacrum and inferiorly to the remaining pelvic inlet, ensuring well-vascularized tissue at the base of the wound.
The surgeon places a suction drain underneath the flap, extending to the base of the perineal defect. The wound is then closed in layers using a standard VY closure technique.

There are a number of important post-operative considerations in patients who have received an eye gap flap. After surgery, a supine position is avoided for the first twenty four hours and sitting is avoided for the first four days. The flaps are reviewed daily by the plastics and colorectal teams. The patients are allowed to mobilise on the first post-operative day as part of an enhanced recovery programme. Patients are placed in a compression garment continuously for two weeks and then during the day for the following two weeks.

The eye gap flap represents a straightforward and reliable option for reconstructing perineal tissue defects. It offers consistent tissue coverage with reduced donor site morbidity, which is important for patients who have often undergone multiple previous operations. The eye gap flap should be an important consideration for coloprotologists who manage patients suffering from significant perineal tissue loss.
